messages.content: improve and make it more academic: To participate in local administration, including public works and public welfare, educational activities, administration of communal self-defense and local militia (local army), etc. A typical example is Zeng Guofan of the late Qing Dynasty organizing the “Xiang Army” in his hometown Hunan as a temporary retiree from the central government in mourning for his father’s death.

To engage in local governance, encompassing initiatives such as public works, public welfare, educational endeavors, and the management of communal self-defense and local militia, demonstrates a commitment to serving one's community. An exemplary instance of this can be seen in Zeng Guofan's leadership during the late Qing Dynasty, when he established the "Xiang Army" in his native province of Hunan while temporarily stepping away from the central government to mourn the passing of his father.

improve and make it more academic:  “In general, conflict of interests occurred between the magistrate and individual members of the gentry rather than the local gentry as a whole” (Ch’ü, 1988, pp. 190-191). As the two parties “belonged to the same privileged class, they depended on each other for the status quo, with the result that they remained entrenched in their perpetuation of their common interests” (Ch’ü, 1988, p. 192). The career and reputation of local officials to a large extent depended on the gentry; and the latter also relied on the former to maintain their privileges and influence in the local community. The cooperation “between the gentry and the magistrate was reversed whenever there was a clash of interests between the two–for example, when the magistrate insisted on enforcing laws that the gentry customarily violated, or when he prevented them from seeking personal gain” (Ch’ü, 1988, p. 191). Local officials were often under the threat of fault-finding, intimidation, slander, and accusations from the gentry. The latter were likewise subject to the stress and strains of the former, particularly in the case of a scholar-gentry, whose status was less secure.

"In the broader context of local governance, conflict of interests primarily emerged between individual members of the gentry and the magistrate, rather than the collective gentry as a whole" (Ch'ü, 1988, pp. 190-191). This can be attributed to their shared privileged class status, which cultivated a symbiotic relationship where they relied on each other to maintain the existing social order, leading to a perpetuation of their mutual interests (Ch'ü, 1988, p. 192). The advancement and reputation of local officials were significantly contingent upon the support and endorsement of the gentry, who, in turn, depended on these officials to uphold their privileges and exert influence within the local community. However, cooperation between the gentry and the magistrate underwent a reversal whenever their interests clashed, such as instances where the magistrate insisted on enforcing laws that the gentry routinely violated or curtailed their pursuit of personal gains (Ch'ü, 1988, p. 191). Consequently, local officials frequently found themselves vulnerable to fault-finding, intimidation, slander, and malicious accusations from the gentry. Similarly, the gentry, particularly the scholar-gentry whose societal position held less security, confronted mounting pressures and strains originating from these officials.

improve and make it more academic: The appointment of officials in the Han Dynasty was primarily through elevation on recommendation  and imperial recruitment , supplemented by examinations and inheritance.

During the Han Dynasty, the selection and appointment of officials were mainly carried out through two primary methods: elevation based on recommendations and imperial recruitment. These approaches were further complemented by the utilization of examinations and inheritance processes.

The elevation of officials through recommendations involved individuals being proposed by influential figures or established officials to be appointed to governmental positions. This method relied heavily on personal connections and patronage networks, enabling those with influential connections to secure positions within the bureaucracy. Moreover, the imperial recruitment system facilitated the selection of candidates directly by the emperor or his delegates. This method aimed to identify talents and individuals with exceptional abilities and qualities through a centralized and merit-based approach.

In addition to these primary methods, the Han Dynasty also employed examinations to assess and evaluate the capabilities and knowledge of potential officials. These examinations aimed to identify and select individuals who demonstrated scholarly aptitude and proficiency in essential subjects such as Confucian classics, legal codes, and administrative principles. Successful candidates who excelled in these examinations were awarded prestigious positions within the government.

Furthermore, the inheritance system played a supplemental role in the appointment of officials during the Han Dynasty. In certain cases, familial ties and hereditary privilege influenced the selection process, as individuals from influential families were often favored for official positions based on their lineage and social status.

Thus, the appointment of officials in the Han Dynasty encompassed a multifaceted approach involving elevation through recommendations, imperial recruitment, examinations, and, to a lesser extent, inheritance, each contributing to the composition of the bureaucratic system.

improve and make it more academic: The commandery quota system (chaju, 察举, literally elevation after examination) originated from the imperial edict to seek talents by Emperor Gaozu of the Han Dynasty in the 11th year of his reign (196 BC); Emperor Hui and Emperor Wen of the Han also respectively issued edicts to recruit talents who were with “filial piety and fraternal duty”, “wise and virtuous, upright and correct”, “frank and unflinching in admonishing the emperor”. In the early reign of Emperor Wu of the Han, commanderies and princedoms were ordered to recommend a “filial and incorrupt” talent per year. (In ancient times, emperors “employed filial piety as a means of governance”, and emperors’ posthumous title usually included the Chinese character xiao, 孝, i.e., “filial piety”.) Later, commanderies and districts were ordered to select from clerks and commoners cultivated talents with extraordinary abilities capable of serving as generals or envoys to distant places. The Eastern Han Dynasty, to avoid the taboo word xiu in Emperor Wu’s name Liu Xiu (刘秀), renamed “xiucai” (秀才) into “maocai” (茂才). The regular path for literati in the Han to enter officialdom was through being recommended as the wise and virtuous, upright and correct, or as the filial and incorrupt, and a lesser path, as xiucai. All of the above were “selected from among villages and hamlets”, supplemented by such examinations as where candidates were required to “answer quickly as arrows go” (shece,射策), or to participate in political discussion (duice,对策). For example, Jia Yi (a famous political commentator in the early Western Han), Gongsun Hong (a prime minister in the Western Han), and Dong Zhongshu (a philosopher and erudite in the in the Western Han), after being recommended, came into the emperor’s favor because of their excellent performance in political discussions. At that time, there were three methods of talent selection, through recommendation by commanderies and princedoms, or dispatching central government officials to “select talents after examination”, or ordering high-ranking ministers to “recommend”.

The commandery quota system, known as chaju (察举) in Chinese, originated during the Han Dynasty under Emperor Gaozu's reign in 196 BC. This system was established through imperial edicts issued by subsequent emperors such as Emperor Hui and Emperor Wen, who sought to recruit individuals displaying "filial piety and fraternal duty," "wisdom and virtue, uprightness and correctness," or those who were "bold and unwavering in advising the emperor." Early in Emperor Wu's reign, commanderies and princedoms were mandated to recommend one individual annually who exemplified both filial piety and incorruptibility. Filial piety, an essential aspect of governance in ancient times, was often associated with imperial posthumous titles, which frequently incorporated the Chinese character "xiao" (孝), meaning "filial piety."

As the Han Dynasty progressed, commanderies and districts began selecting talented individuals with exceptional abilities from clerks and commoners who demonstrated potential as military generals or envoys to distant regions. During the Eastern Han Dynasty, the term "xiucai" (秀才), which denoted individuals with excellent literary skills, was later renamed as "maocai" (茂才) to avoid the taboo associated with the character "xiu" in Emperor Wu's name, Liu Xiu (刘秀).

The primary pathway for literati in the Han Dynasty to enter official positions was through recommendations based on their reputation for wisdom, virtue, uprightness, correctness, or filial piety and incorruptibility. A less prominent means of entry was through the title of "xiucai." These selections were predominantly made from individuals residing in villages and hamlets. Supplementary examinations, such as the "shece" (射策), which required candidates to provide quick and accurate responses akin to arrows, or the "duice" (对策), which involved participating in political discussions, were also utilized. Distinguished figures such as Jia Yi, a renowned political commentator in the early Western Han Dynasty, Gongsun Hong, a prime minister in the Western Han Dynasty, and Dong Zhongshu, a philosopher and erudite during the Western Han Dynasty, gained imperial favor after being recommended due to their impressive performances in political discussions.

Overall, talent selection during this period followed three main methods: recommendations by commanderies and princedoms, dispatching central government officials to conduct examinations for talent identification, or high-ranking ministers providing recommendations.

improve and make it more academic: The method of internal recommendation started in the Western Han Dynasty and prevailed in the Eastern Han. Different from recommendation from among villages and hamlets, where candidates needed to advance step by step, candidates through internal recommendation could be promoted overstepping their proper qualification or rank. In recruitment and appointment upon imperial order (zheng, 征), the emperor directly invited highly talented people, but not all of those invited would be appointed. In elevation or appointment by His Majesty (bi, 辟), talents were appointed to offices at administrative institutions and (prefectures), counties, which would inspect their behaviors and abilities and appoint or promote them upon vacancy in turn, with some candidates even advanced to high-ranking posts of 2000 bushels (Sa, 1975, pp. 306-307).

The practice of internal recommendation, originating in the Western Han Dynasty and persisting during the Eastern Han Dynasty, differed from the village and hamlet-based recommendations, as it allowed candidates to bypass the traditional progression and ascend beyond their designated qualifications or ranks. This method provided opportunities for candidates to be promoted based on their exceptional abilities, regardless of their current standing. In the recruitment and appointment process conducted by imperial order (zheng, 征), the emperor personally extended invitations to individuals showcasing remarkable talent. However, it should be noted that not all invitees would ultimately receive appointments.

Within the framework of elevation or appointment by the emperor (bi, 辟), talented individuals were chosen to serve in administrative institutions, prefectures, and counties. Their behaviors and capabilities were assessed during their tenure, and subsequent promotions or appointments were made as vacancies arose. In some cases, these candidates were even granted access to high-ranking positions equivalent to 2000 bushels (a measurement of grain storage) (Sa, 1975, pp. 306-307).

improve and make it more academic: The Wei, Jin, Southern and Northern Dynasties adopted the “Nine-rank Selection Method”. In the Han, when the responsibility of talent recommendation gradually transferred from local officials to retired scholar-bureaucrats, malpractice gradually emerged, resulting in the phenomena of “cultivated talents not knowing the Four Books; filial and incorrupt talents failing to take care of their fathers”. To prevent such ill practice as in the Han, the Wei and Jin adopted the “Nine-rank Selection Method”. At the chaotic end of the Han when Cao Cao (a statesman and warlord during the Three Kingdoms period) seized power, the nine-rank system was established in the army to calibrate the quality of talents. When Emperor Wen of the Wei came to power, the “nine-rank officials” method was used to place senior and junior rectifiers (zhongzheng, 中正) in each province and commandery in order to choose and place talents into corresponding ranks accordingly. At the beginning, junior rectifiers at the commandery and county level reported the selected talents to senior rectifiers at prefectures, who checked the list and reported to the Minister of Education in the central government. Afterwards, the list would then be submitted to the grand ministers for final decision. The nine-rank system was intended to overcome the flaws of the recommendation system. However, its own demerits began to be increasingly pronounced since the Southern Dynasty. For one thing, the harm of pulling strings overtook that of fishing for fame in the recommendation system. Moreover, there were no established ranking criteria. At that time, the power of great gentry-families boomed, and family rather than capability determined the rank of candidates for offices. Thus, there is a ridicule of the situation saying that “no scion of a poor family was in high offices, and no son of an eminent family had a humble post”. When it came to the reign of Emperor Wen of the Sui, “the tradition of talent recommendation by prefectures, commanderies and counties was cancelled, and the appointment of officials became the responsibility of the Ministry of Personnel.” Thus far, the recommendation system was abolished.

During the Wei, Jin, Southern, and Northern Dynasties, a system known as the "Nine-rank Selection Method" was implemented. This approach aimed to address the issues that had emerged during the Han Dynasty when the responsibility of talent recommendation gradually shifted from local officials to retired scholar-bureaucrats. As a result, malpractices arose, leading to instances where cultivated talents lacked knowledge of the Four Books or filial and incorrupt talents neglected their familial obligations.

To prevent such deficiencies, the Wei and Jin Dynasties introduced the "Nine-rank Selection Method". During the tumultuous end of the Han Dynasty when Cao Cao seized power, the nine-rank system was established within the military to evaluate the quality of talents. Under the reign of Emperor Wen of the Wei Dynasty, the "nine-rank officials" method was used to allocate senior and junior rectifiers (zhongzheng, 中正) in provinces and commanderies. Their role was to identify and assign talents to corresponding ranks based on their abilities. Initially, junior rectifiers at the commandery and county levels reported their selections to senior rectifiers at the prefecture level. The senior rectifiers reviewed the lists and reported to the Minister of Education in the central government. The final decision on appointments was made by the grand ministers.

The purpose of the nine-rank system was to overcome the flaws of the recommendation system. However, it began to exhibit its own shortcomings during the Southern Dynasty. Firstly, the detrimental practice of exerting personal influence exceeded the negative aspects of seeking fame through recommendations. Additionally, there were no established criteria for ranking. During this period, the power of influential gentry families increased, and a candidate's social standing, rather than their capabilities, determined their appointment rank. Consequently, a common critique emerged, stating that individuals from humble backgrounds seldom achieved high-ranking positions, while those from esteemed families rarely held lowly posts.

With the advent of Emperor Wen's reign in the Sui Dynasty, the tradition of talent recommendation by prefectures, commanderies, and counties was abolished. Instead, the Ministry of Personnel assumed responsibility for official appointments. Thus, the recommendation system was officially abolished during this period.
After the Sui and Tang Dynasties, imperial examination flourished, but there were also parallel special or irregular examinations, i.e., the emperor directly issuing edits to recruit talents.

Following the Sui and Tang Dynasties, the system of imperial examination experienced significant growth and prominence. However, alongside the regular examination process, there were parallel special or irregular examinations that took place. These special examinations involved the emperor directly issuing edicts to recruit talented individuals.

While the regular imperial examination was a standardized and formalized process, the special examinations provided an alternative means for talent recruitment. In these cases, the emperor would personally intervene and issue edicts to identify and select individuals with exceptional abilities, knowledge, or specialized skills. These special examinations were conducted outside of the usual examination schedule and often targeted specific areas of expertise or urgent requirements for the imperial court.

The purpose of these special examinations was to supplement the regular examination system, ensuring that the empire had access to individuals with specific talents or expertise when the need arose. By directly recruiting candidates through imperial edicts, the emperor had greater control over the selection process and could prioritize individuals based on immediate or specialized requirements.

Thus, while the imperial examination system provided a standardized method for talent recruitment, the parallel special examination process allowed for flexibility and targeted recruitment based on the emperor's direct intervention.

improve and make it more academic: In the first years of Emperor Yang of the Sui, the emperor ordered officials above the fifth rank to recommend candidates of different categories, renowned for filial piety and fraternal duty, honesty and sincerity, persistence in law enforcement, excellent academic performance, literary talents, or abilities to serve as good generals, and so on. Therefore, although the Sui abolished the old recommendation system, it established the institution of special examinations, which was adopted by later dynasties.

During the early years of Emperor Yang's reign in the Sui Dynasty, a significant change occurred in the talent selection process. The emperor issued a decree ordering officials above the fifth rank to recommend candidates from various categories. These categories included individuals renowned for their filial piety and fraternal duty, commitment to honesty and sincerity, their persistence in law enforcement, remarkable academic achievements, literary talents, or exceptional abilities to serve as effective military generals, among others. 

As a result, while the Sui Dynasty abolished the previous recommendation system, it introduced the institution of special examinations, which would have a lasting impact on subsequent dynasties.

The special examinations served as an alternative approach to talent selection, distinct from the regular standardized examination system. These examinations were designed to target specific qualities, skills, or expertise deemed essential for the empire's administration and security. By focusing on different categories, the Sui Dynasty aimed to identify individuals who possessed exceptional traits or capabilities beyond the scope of what the regular examination process evaluated. 

The institution of special examinations introduced during the Sui Dynasty laid the foundation for its adoption and continued use in subsequent dynasties. This system allowed the ruling authorities to diversify their talent pool by directly identifying and recruiting individuals who excelled in specific areas of importance to the imperial court and the governance of the empire.

improve and make it more academic: Following the talent selection practice of the Sui, the Tang resorted to special examinations to supplement the imperial examinations and recruited people of capabilities and integrity with a straight tongue, learned experts in ancient classics and able to educate the people, and men of extraordinary military talent to serve as generals, etc.
Building on the talent selection practices introduced during the Sui Dynasty, the Tang Dynasty incorporated special examinations as a complementary system to the regular
